summary: DHH explores the difficult question of when to abandon a failing endeavor rather than persisting. He argues that while 'never give up' is a good default, the most dangerous trap isn't total failure but partial success — things that sorta-kinda work but aren't worth the continued investment. He uses Rails system tests as a concrete example, where 37signals recently deleted 5,000 lines of system tests from Basecamp after years of trying to make the approach work at scale. He draws parallels between startups, development projects, and societal policies, suggesting time boxing as a practical antidote and concluding that the right time to quit is probably sooner than you think.
key_points: The most dangerous trap isn't outright failure but partial success — ideas that sorta-kinda work keep people invested far longer than they should be
Even the best VCs are wrong more often than right because nobody can consistently predict disruption, making the decision of when to fold inherently difficult
Time boxing (like Shape Up's six-week cycles) provides a hard stop that counteracts the human tendency to throw good effort after bad
The ability to fold and preserve remaining resources applies universally — to startups, projects, methodologies, and even societal policies
Knowing when to give up requires humility, and the right answer is almost always 'less than you think, less than you want'
money_quotes: The trouble was that it sorta-kinda did work! Some of the time. With great effort. But ultimately wasn't worth the squeeze.
Having the grit to stick with the effort when the going gets hard is a key trait of successful people. But having the humility to give up on good bets turned bad might be just as important.
Stuck with an idea that sorta-kinda does work, but not well enough to be worth a decade of their life. That's a tragic trap.
The only antidote I've found to this on the development side is time boxing.
Whatever the answer, it's 'less than you think, less than you want'.
The idea ought to look a bit daft at first glance or it probably doesn't carry the counter-intuitive kernel needed to really pop.
Programmers are just as liable as anyone to believe a flawed design can work if given just a bit more time. And then a bit more. And then just double of what we've already spent.
key_insight: The most dangerous failure mode isn't something that doesn't work at all, but something that sorta-kinda works — just enough to keep you trapped far longer than the investment is worth.
